display_name: STP topology change notifications after a link event are normal convergence, not a loop
context: Syslog or show spanning-tree output showing topology change notifications (TCN) or TC flag after a link goes up or down
observation: Multiple STP topology change notifications appearing in logs after a link event
naive_interpretation: There is a spanning tree loop or instability in the network
correct_interpretation: A burst of TCNs immediately following a link state change is STP doing its job — reconverging the tree after a topology event. This is expected and healthy behavior. A loop would manifest as sustained TCN storms, MAC flapping on multiple ports, and high CPU — not a one-time burst that settles within the convergence timer window (typically 2-30 seconds for RSTP).
why: STP is designed to send TCNs when topology changes — that is how it propagates the change to all bridges
Each bridge receiving the TCN shortens its MAC aging timer to flush stale entries, which is correct behavior
RSTP converges in 1-2 seconds; legacy STP takes up to 50 seconds. TCNs during this window are normal

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check the topology change counter and when the last change occurred — a single recent timestamp is normal, rapid-fire changes over minutes is not
Check topology change count and timestamp
If MAC table is fluctuating wildly over time, that suggests a real loop — a stable count after the TCN burst is normal

traps: Seeing TCN logs and assuming there is a loop without checking whether the TCNs settled
Disabling STP or portfast to stop the TCN messages instead of understanding they are normal
Not distinguishing between a one-time TCN burst (normal convergence) and sustained TCN storms (actual problem)
